title: 16 Hidar — St. John the Baptist & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "16 Hidar — St. John the Baptist & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"16 Hidar — St. John the Baptist & The Assembly

narrative\": \"Hidar

St. John the Baptist (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Tatian the Martyr

St. Ananias of Damascus, Apostle of the Seventy

The sixteenth day of Hidar opens the month's second half with the monthly commemoration of St. John the Baptist — the Forerunner, the Voice in the Wilderness, the one whose entire life was the apostolic vesting in its most singular and most costly expression. John did not choose his commission; he was filled with the Holy Spirit from his mother's womb, formed in the desert, and sent to the Jordan with the single task of making the way straight. His apostolic vesting was the most precise in all of scripture: one man, one mission, one river, one season. He prepared the way with his own body — his austere life, his public proclamation, his willingness to lose his disciples to the One who came after him, his imprisonment, his execution. In Hidar's apostolic month, John at the beginning of the second half is the image of the commission that does not flinch from the place where it leads — the Forerunner who goes ahead of the community into the places the community has not yet reached.

St. Tatian the Martyr brings the young woman's apostolic witness into the sixteenth day's assembly — one of the company of early female martyrs whose witness was given in the specific form of the refusal to surrender what had been received at baptism. The vesting that the font provided was the vesting that the imperial decree demanded be returned, and Tatian's refusal to return it is the apostolic act that Hidar recognizes: the maintenance of the commission under conditions designed to make its surrender seem reasonable, even prudent. In the second half of the apostolic month, Tatian stands as the testimony that the vesting's maintenance is itself a form of mission — that the baptized life held faithfully under pressure is as genuinely apostolic as any public proclamation.

St. Ananias of Damascus is one of the most humanly moving figures in the entire Acts of the Apostles — the disciple who received the terrifying instruction to go to the street called Straight and find the man who had been persecuting the Church, who expressed the very reasonable objection that this man had been doing great harm to the Lord's people, and who went anyway when the Lord explained the plan. Ananias laid his hands on Saul of Tarsus, restored his sight, baptized him, and disappeared from the narrative — reappearing only in Paul's later account of his conversion. He is the patron of those whose apostolic commission required them to go to the person they most feared, to extend the hand of blessing to the one whose arrival they most dreaded, and to trust that the One who sent them had already arranged what they would find when they arrived.

John the Baptist, Tatian, and Ananias together in the second half of Hidar present the apostolic vesting in three of its most challenging operational modes. John goes ahead into the wilderness and the prison cell, making the way straight. Tatian holds the vesting under imperial pressure, refusing the exchange that would purchase safety at the cost of the commission. Ananias crosses the street to the enemy's address, trusting the instruction he has received. These are the apostolic acts of the month's second half — not the triumphant proclamations of the feast days but the specific, costly, humanly frightening decisions that the vesting requires in the ordinary course of the commissioned life.
